rationale: Avoid defining patients by their condition. Use person-first language (AMA Style).

Stigmatizing term. Use precise medical terminology.

Stigmatizing term.
Avoid defining patients by their disease.
In clinical care writing and case reports, prefer 'patient'. In research methodology and AI/computational studies, 'subject' is widely accepted — flag only as a minor suggestion, not an error.
In clinical care writing, prefer 'patients'. In research and methodology papers, 'subjects' is acceptable.
Avoid emotional language. Use neutral, objective descriptions.
Avoid emotional language. Use neutral, objective descriptions.
Focus on the person and the assistive device neutrally.
'Non-compliant' is paternalistic. 'Non-adherent' reflects a failure of the treatment plan, not just the patient.
'Expired' applies to medications, not patients. Use clear, objective terminology (died).
'Complaint' can have negative connotations in patient-facing contexts. In academic methods papers this term is standard — flag only in clinical writing.
Specify the exact role. 'Provider' is considered overly generic and corporate by many medical societies.
Studies rarely 'prove' anything definitively. Use more scientifically accurate hedging.
Studies rarely 'prove' anything definitively. Use more scientifically accurate hedging.
In observational studies, 'impact' implies a causal relationship not supported by the design. Acceptable in RCTs and experimental studies.
In observational studies, 'impacts' implies causation. Acceptable in RCTs.
Do not use 'cause' unless the study is a rigorously designed RCT.
Do not use 'causes' unless the study is an RCT.
Reserve 'significant' ONLY for 'statistically significant' (p<0.05). If describing size or importance, use alternatives.
Reserve 'significantly' ONLY for statistical contexts. Use alternatives for magnitude.
Subjective, promotional adjective. Let the literature review establish the novelty.
Subjective and promotional. Remove from academic prose.
Subjective and promotional. Remove from academic prose.
'Reference standard' is preferred in diagnostic accuracy studies (STARD/TRIPOD).
An outcome is either significant or it is not. A 'trend' is not 'statistically significant'.

suggested_replacement: trend toward significance / p-value of X
rationale: Report the exact p-value rather than using subjective phrases.
